title: Exponentials & Logarithms
blurb: Growth that multiplies instead of adds, the compounding that leads to e, and logarithms — the inverse that turns multiplication into addition and solves for exponents.
buildsOn: Exponent rules from Algebra 1; inverse functions from Unit 1.

lessons.title: Exponential Growth & Decay
lessons.concreteComparison: Linear growth is a salary: +500 every month, same step forever. Exponential growth is a rumor: everyone who hears it tells two friends — the step itself grows, and what starts slower ends unimaginably faster.
lessons.objective: Model with f(x) = a·bˣ: identify initial value and growth/decay factor, convert percent rates to factors, and compare exponential with linear growth.
concept: The exponential family is $f(x) = a \cdot b^x$ with $a$ the **initial value** ($f(0) = a$, the y-intercept) and $b$ the **factor** applied once per step. $b > 1$: growth. $0 < b < 1$: decay. Each step MULTIPLIES where linear growth adds.
Percent language converts to factors: '+12% per year' means $b = 1.12$; '−25% per year' means $b = 0.75$. The factor is $1 + r$ with $r$ signed — never the percent alone, always 'one plus'.

Every exponential $a \cdot b^x$ (with $a > 0$) has the same skeleton: y-intercept at $a$, horizontal asymptote $y = 0$ on one side (decay side), unbounded blast-off on the other. It NEVER touches zero: halving forever never reaches nothing.
lessons.keyIdea: f(x) = a·bˣ: start at a, multiply by b each step — b = 1 + r from percent language; growth beats any line eventually, decay halves toward the y = 0 asymptote forever.

facts.title: Anatomy
facts.explanation: Start value and constant ratio — the two dials.

facts.title: Percent to factor
facts.explanation: b = 1 + r, rate signed; the '1 +' is mandatory.

facts.title: The floor
facts.explanation: Repeated multiplication by a positive number never lands on zero.

workedExamples.statement: A colony starts at 500 bacteria and grows 20% per hour. Write the model and find the population after 3 hours.
A car worth $20{,}000$ loses 15% of its value each year. Model its value and compute it after 2 years.

commonMistakes.correction: The factor is what REMAINS: $b = 1 - 0.15 = 0.85$. Using 0.15 models losing 85% a year. Always compute 1 + r first, sign included.

commonMistakes.text: Adding the growth each step: 500, 600, 720 → writing 500 + 100t.
commonMistakes.correction: 20% growth multiplies: $500 \to 600 \to 720$ has RATIO 1.2, not equal steps (100 then 120). Check consecutive values by dividing, not subtracting — constant ratio means exponential.

tryIt.statement: A table shows $g(0) = 6, g(1) = 18, g(2) = 54$. Find the formula and $g(4)$.
tryIt.solution: Ratios: $\frac{18}{6} = 3 = \frac{54}{18}$: constant, so exponential with $a = 6, b = 3$: $g(x) = 6 \cdot 3^x$. $g(4) = 6 \cdot 81 = 486$.

tryIt.statement: Medicine decays 50% every hour in the bloodstream. Starting from 80 mg, how much remains after 4 hours — and does it ever reach exactly 0?
tryIt.solution: $M(t) = 80(0.5)^t$; $M(4) = 80 \cdot \frac{1}{16} = 5$ mg. It never reaches 0: each hour halves what's left, and half of a positive amount is positive — the graph rides the asymptote $y = 0$ forever.

steps.title: Adding vs multiplying
steps.body: Two ways to grow. A salary adds: $+500$ each month, the same step forever — that's LINEAR. A rumor multiplies: each teller recruits two more — the step itself grows. Exponential functions are the algebra of the second kind: $f(x) = a \cdot b^x$, where each unit of time MULTIPLIES by the factor $b$.

steps.title: The two dials: a and b
steps.teach: Step the start value $a$ and the base $b$. The ×b chips along the curve show the signature: every step right multiplies by the same $b$. Push $b$ below 1 and growth turns to decay — the same machine, shrinking instead.

steps.title: Spot the exponential
steps.prompt: Which table is exponential? A: $4, 8, 12, 16$. B: $4, 8, 16, 32$.
steps.explanation: Test by DIVIDING consecutive terms: B gives $2, 2, 2$ (exponential, $b = 2$); A gives $2, 1.5, 1.33$ — no. A has constant DIFFERENCES: linear.

steps.title: The factor is 1 + r
steps.body: Real problems speak in percents: 'grows 20% per hour', 'loses 15% per year'. Convert BEFORE modeling: the factor is $b = 1 + r$ with the rate signed. $+20\% \to b = 1.2$; $-15\% \to b = 0.85$. The '1 +' carries the part that stays; the single most common exponential error is dropping it.

steps.title: Read a model
options: starts at 300 and loses 6% per step
starts at 300 and loses 94% per step
steps.explanation: $b = 0.94 = 1 - 0.06$: each step keeps 94%, losing 6%. Read decay factors as 'what remains'.

steps.title: y = 6·3ˣ, sampled
steps.teach: Samples of the tryIt function $6 \cdot 3^x$: left of the y-axis it hugs the floor $y = 0$ (at $x = -2$ it's only $\frac{2}{3}$), passes its intercept $(0, 6)$, then detonates — 18, 54. The flat-left, explosive-right silhouette is every growth exponential.

steps.title: Halving never reaches nothing
steps.body: Decay's destination deserves a close look: $80 \to 40 \to 20 \to 10 \to 5 \to \ldots$ Each hour halves what remains — and half of a positive number is still positive. The graph approaches the x-axis forever without landing: $y = 0$ is a horizontal asymptote, the exponential's only floor. (Shifted models $a b^x + c$ move the floor to $y = c$.)

steps.title: The chessboard bill
steps.body: The classic: one grain of rice on square 1, doubling each square. Square 64 carries $2^{63} \approx 9.2 \times 10^{18}$ grains — more rice than humanity has EVER harvested. Constant-ratio growth is genuinely beyond intuition; that's why we model it instead of guessing.

steps.title: What sticks
points: f(x) = a·bˣ: start a, constant ratio b — test tables by dividing, not subtracting.
Percent to factor: b = 1 + r, rate signed; decay keeps what remains.
Exponentials never touch 0: y = 0 is the horizontal asymptote.

lessons.title: Compound Interest & the Number e
lessons.concreteComparison: Compounding is interest earning interest — a snowball picking up snow that itself picks up snow. Squeeze the compounding intervals tighter and tighter and the formula doesn't blow up; it converges to nature's own growth constant, e ≈ 2.718.
lessons.objective: Apply the compound interest formula for n compoundings per year, see how more frequent compounding approaches the limit e, and use continuous growth A = Pe^(rt).
concept: Compounding $n$ times per year at annual rate $r$ splits the rate into $n$ slices of $\frac{r}{n}$, applied as factors: $A = P\left(1 + \frac{r}{n}\right)^{nt}$. More slices beat fewer: 10% compounded twice gives $(1.05)^2 = 1.1025$ — a quarter percent better than 10% once, because mid-year interest starts earning too.

Push $n \to \infty$ and the sequence $\left(1 + \frac{1}{n}\right)^n$ — yearly 2, monthly 2.613, daily 2.7146 — climbs toward a ceiling: **$e \approx 2.71828$**, the natural base. Continuous compounding is the limit: $A = Pe^{rt}$.

$e$ is not a curiosity — it's the base nature prefers: population growth, radioactive decay, cooling coffee, and (later) calculus, where $e^x$ is the unique function that is its own derivative. Most scientific models arrive written as $Q(t) = Q_0 e^{kt}$: growth for $k > 0$, decay for $k < 0$.
lessons.keyIdea: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt): more compounding slices always help but converge — the limit is e ≈ 2.718 and continuous growth A = Pe^(rt).

facts.title: n compoundings
facts.explanation: Split the rate into n slices; apply each as a factor.

facts.title: The limit
facts.explanation: More slices converge — they don't blow up.

facts.title: Continuous model
facts.explanation: The n → ∞ limit; nature's default growth law.

workedExamples.statement: $1000$ is invested at 8% for one year: compare annual vs quarterly compounding.
workedExamples.solution: Annual: $1000(1.08) = 1080$. Quarterly: four slices of 2%: $1000(1.02)^4 = 1000 \cdot 1.08243216 = 1082.43$ — quarterly wins by about $2.43$, the interest earned by mid-year interest.

workedExamples.statement: A population grows continuously: $P(t) = 2000 e^{0.05t}$. What is the initial population, the continuous rate, and the population's factor over one year (3 decimal places, using $e^{0.05} \approx 1.05127$)?
workedExamples.solution: Initial: $P(0) = 2000e^0 = 2000$. Continuous rate 5%. Yearly factor: $e^{0.05} \approx 1.051$ — slightly MORE than 5.0% effective growth, the continuous-compounding bonus.

commonMistakes.text: Writing quarterly compounding of 8% as (1.08)⁴ per year.
commonMistakes.correction: Slice the RATE, not just the count: each quarter applies $\frac{8\%}{4} = 2\%$, so the year is $(1.02)^4 \approx 1.0824$ — not $(1.08)^4 \approx 1.36$, which would be 8% four times over.

commonMistakes.text: Expecting infinite compounding to produce infinite money.
commonMistakes.correction: The slices shrink as fast as they multiply: $(1 + \frac{1}{n})^n$ climbs but converges to $e \approx 2.718$. Continuous compounding of 100% yearly turns 1 into about 2.72, not into infinity.

tryIt.statement: $500$ at 6% for 2 years, compounded semiannually: compute the amount.
tryIt.solution: Four half-year slices of 3%: $A = 500(1.03)^4 = 500 \cdot 1.12550881 = 562.75$ (to the cent).

tryIt.statement: Carbon dating uses $Q(t) = Q_0 e^{-0.000121 t}$. Is this growth or decay, and what fraction remains after one half-life cycle if $e^{-0.000121 \cdot 5730} \approx 0.4998$?
tryIt.solution: Negative exponent coefficient: DECAY. After $t = 5730$ years the factor is $\approx 0.4998 \approx \frac{1}{2}$ — half remains. That's why 5730 years is called carbon-14's half-life.

steps.title: Interest earning interest
steps.body: Simple interest pays on the principal only. **Compound** interest pays on the whole running balance — including past interest. The mid-year interest starts earning immediately, like a snowball whose new snow itself gathers snow. The natural question: does compounding MORE OFTEN always help? And by how much?

steps.title: Watch a balance compound
steps.teach: A principal compounds year by year — each bar is the last bar times the same factor. Notice the bars' GROWTH grows: the increments themselves climb, because interest is earning interest. That widening gap is the whole difference from simple interest.

steps.title: Slice the rate into n pieces
steps.body: Compounding $n$ times a year at annual rate $r$: each period applies the SLICED rate $\frac{r}{n}$, and a year runs $n$ periods: $A = P(1 + \frac{r}{n})^{nt}$. Quarterly 8% means four multiplications by $1.02$ — NOT four by $1.08$. Slice first, then count periods.

steps.title: Set up the slices
steps.prompt: $2000$ at 12% for 3 years, compounded monthly. The correct expression is:
steps.explanation: Slice: $\frac{12\%}{12} = 1\%$ per month. Count: $12 \times 3 = 36$ periods. $(1.01)^{36}$ — slice the rate AND multiply the periods.

steps.title: Squeezing to e
steps.body: Take 100% annual growth and slice ever finer: yearly $(1+1)^1 = 2$; monthly $(1 + \frac{1}{12})^{12} \approx 2.613$; daily $\approx 2.7146$; hourly $\approx 2.71813$. The gains shrink — the sequence converges to $e \approx 2.71828$, the **natural base**. Infinite compounding buys you $e$, not infinity. The continuous-growth formula is the limit made law: $A = Pe^{rt}$.

steps.title: The ceiling
steps.explanation: More slices help less and less: 2, 2.61, 2.71, 2.718… The ceiling is $e$ — one of mathematics' fundamental constants, born from this exact squeeze.

steps.title: Nature's default base
steps.body: Any base works for algebra, but science writes its models in base $e$: populations $Q_0e^{kt}$, radioactive decay $Q_0e^{-kt}$, cooling coffee, charging capacitors. The deep reason arrives in calculus — $e^x$ is the unique function equal to its own rate of change — but you'll meet $e$ on every calculator and in every textbook NOW, so treat it as a first-class base: just a number, $\approx 2.718$.

steps.title: Sanity-band every answer
steps.body: Compound answers live in a predictable band: MORE than simple interest, LESS than continuous. For 8% on 1000: simple 1080, continuous $1000e^{0.08} \approx 1083.29$. Any quarterly/monthly answer must land between — outside the band means a slicing error, instantly caught.

steps.title: What sticks
points: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt): slice the rate, count the periods.
Finer slicing helps less and less: the limit is e ≈ 2.718, and A = Pe^(rt).
Science defaults to base e; k's sign says growth or decay.

lessons.title: Logarithms as Inverses
lessons.concreteComparison: A logarithm answers the question an exponential leaves hanging: the exponential says 'give me time, I'll give you the amount'; the log runs the machine backwards — 'here's the amount, tell me the time.' log₂ 8 just asks: 2 to WHAT power is 8?
lessons.objective: Convert between exponential and logarithmic form, evaluate logs mentally, use the product/quotient/power rules, and graph y = log_b x as the mirror of y = bˣ.
concept: $\log_b y = x$ **means** $b^x = y$ — the same fact, arrow reversed. $\log_2 8 = 3$ because $2^3 = 8$. A logarithm IS an exponent: the exponent you'd need. Special names: $\log$ (base 10) and $\ln$ (base $e$).

As the inverse of $y = b^x$, the graph of $y = \log_b x$ is its mirror across $y = x$ (Unit 1!): domain and range swap, so logs accept only POSITIVE inputs and output all reals; the exponential's horizontal asymptote $y = 0$ becomes the log's vertical asymptote $x = 0$; the anchor $(0, 1)$ flips to $(1, 0)$: $\log_b 1 = 0$ always.

Because logs are exponents, exponent rules become log rules: $\log_b(MN) = \log_b M + \log_b N$ (products turn into sums), $\log_b \frac{M}{N} = \log_b M - \log_b N$, and $\log_b(M^p) = p \log_b M$ — the power rule that will unlock equation-solving next lesson.
lessons.keyIdea: log_b y is THE exponent x with bˣ = y; its graph mirrors the exponential across y = x (domain x > 0, asymptote x = 0), and exponent rules become the product, quotient, and power rules.

facts.title: Definition
facts.explanation: A log is an exponent; convert to exponential form to evaluate.

facts.title: Mirror graph
facts.explanation: Everything about bˣ, reflected across y = x.

facts.title: Three rules
facts.latex: \log MN = \log M + \log N, \; \log \tfrac{M}{N} = \log M - \log N, \; \log M^p = p\log M

workedExamples.solution: Each asks 'base to what power?'. $3^4 = 81$: answer 4. $5^{-2} = \frac{1}{25}$: answer $-2$. $10^0 = 1$: answer 0. $e^7$: the exponent is sitting there — 7.

commonMistakes.text: Expanding log(M + N) as log M + log N.
commonMistakes.correction: The product rule turns MULTIPLICATION into addition: $\log(MN) = \log M + \log N$. There is NO rule for $\log(M + N)$ — it simplifies to nothing. Sums inside a log stay put.

commonMistakes.text: Evaluating log of a negative: log₂(−8) = −3.
commonMistakes.correction: $2^x$ is positive for EVERY real $x$ — no exponent produces $-8$. Logs of negatives (and of 0) are undefined; the domain is strictly $x > 0$. ($-3$ answers a different question: $\log_2 \frac{1}{8}$.)

tryIt.statement: Expand $\log_b \dfrac{x^3 \sqrt{y}}{z}$ into separate logs, then verify your expansion at $b = 10, x = 10, y = 100, z = 10$.
tryIt.solution: $3\log_b x + \frac{1}{2}\log_b y - \log_b z$. Verify: original: $\log_{10} \frac{1000 \cdot 10}{10} = \log_{10} 1000 = 3$; expansion: $3(1) + \frac{1}{2}(2) - 1 = 3$. ✓

steps.title: The question exponentials can't answer
steps.body: $f(t) = 2^t$ answers 'after $t$ doublings, how much?'. But most real questions run BACKWARDS: 'how long until 8×?' You need the inverse machine — and Unit 1 taught you exactly what that means. The inverse of the exponential is the **logarithm**: $\log_2 8$ asks '2 to WHAT power gives 8?' Answer: 3. A log is an exponent.

steps.title: Translate both ways
steps.prompt: Which pair says the SAME fact twice?
steps.explanation: $\log_b y = x \iff b^x = y$: base stays the base, the log's OUTPUT is the exponent. Conversion is the universal log-evaluation move.

steps.title: The exponential in a mirror
steps.body: Since $\log_b x$ inverts $b^x$, its graph is the exponential reflected across $y = x$ — and every feature flips with it. Domain/range SWAP: logs accept only $x > 0$, output all reals. The exponential's floor $y = 0$ tips upright into the log's WALL $x = 0$ (vertical asymptote). The anchor $(0, 1)$ flips to $(1, 0)$: every log sends 1 to 0.

steps.title: y = 2ˣ and y = log₂x, mirrored
steps.teach: Both curves sampled together: each orange exponential point $(a, b)$ has its log partner $(b, a)$ across the invisible diagonal $y = x$. The exponential hugs the floor going left; the log hugs the wall going down — the same tail, reflected.

steps.title: Forbidden inputs
steps.explanation: No power of 2 is negative — the log's domain is $x > 0$. The others: $-2$, $0$, $\frac{1}{2}$: negative OUTPUTS are fine; negative INPUTS never.

steps.title: Exponent laws, log clothing
steps.body: Logs ARE exponents, so exponent laws translate directly. $b^m b^n = b^{m+n}$ becomes the **product rule**: $\log_b(MN) = \log_b M + \log_b N$ — multiplication downstairs is addition upstairs. Division becomes subtraction, and $\left(b^m\right)^p = b^{mp}$ becomes the **power rule**: $\log_b(M^p) = p \log_b M$ — exponents inside hop out front. These three rules are the entire toolkit.

steps.title: Logs built the modern world
steps.body: Before calculators, every serious multiplication ran through log tables: to multiply two numbers, ADD their logs and convert back — the product rule as technology. Slide rules mechanized it, and engineers designed everything from bridges to Apollo trajectories sliding logs past each other.

steps.title: When stuck, convert
steps.body: Any log expression that resists you: write it in exponential form immediately. $\log_9 3 = ?$ becomes $9^? = 3$, and suddenly it's an exponent puzzle you've solved since Algebra 1 ($9^{1/2} = 3$). The definition is the escape hatch, every time.

steps.title: What sticks
points: log_b y = x ⟺ bˣ = y: a log IS an exponent; convert to evaluate.
Graph: the exponential mirrored over y = x — domain x > 0, wall at x = 0, anchor (1, 0).
Products → sums, quotients → differences, powers → out front; log(M + N) has no rule.

lessons.title: Exponential & Logarithmic Equations
lessons.concreteComparison: Solving 3ˣ = 20 without logs is like measuring a coastline with a meter stick — you can bracket the answer (between 2 and 3) but never name it. Taking a log of both sides is the surveyor's laser: x = log 20 / log 3, exact, in one shot.
lessons.objective: Solve exponential equations by matching bases or taking logarithms, solve log equations by converting to exponential form, and check for extraneous solutions.
concept: **Matching bases** handles the tidy cases: $2^{3x} = 16 = 2^4$ forces $3x = 4$ (exponentials are one-to-one, so equal outputs mean equal exponents). Rewrite both sides over one base whenever possible — 4, 8, 16 are all powers of 2.

When bases won't match — $3^x = 20$ — take a log of both sides and pull the exponent down with the power rule: $x \log 3 = \log 20$, so $x = \frac{\log 20}{\log 3} \approx 2.727$. Any log base works; your calculator's $\log$ or $\ln$ both do the job.

**Log equations** convert to exponential form: $\log_2(x + 3) = 4$ means $x + 3 = 2^4 = 16$, so $x = 13$. One mandatory ritual: CHECK every answer in the original — log equations can produce **extraneous solutions** that ask for the log of a negative, and those must be discarded.
lessons.keyIdea: Match bases when possible (equal bases ⇒ equal exponents); otherwise log both sides and pull the power down; log equations convert to exponential form — and every log answer gets checked for domain.

facts.title: One-to-one
facts.explanation: Match the bases, equate the exponents.

facts.title: Log both sides
facts.explanation: The power rule pulls x down; any base of log works.

facts.title: Extraneous check
facts.explanation: Solving can create answers the original equation forbids.

workedExamples.solution: Product rule: $\log_2(x(x-6)) = 4$, so $x^2 - 6x = 16$: $x^2 - 6x - 16 = (x-8)(x+2) = 0$, candidates $8$ and $-2$. CHECK: $x = 8$: $\log_2 8 + \log_2 2 = 3 + 1 = 4$ ✓. $x = -2$: $\log_2(-2)$ — undefined: EXTRANEOUS. Solution: $x = 8$ only.

commonMistakes.text: Keeping the extraneous root because 'the algebra was correct.'
commonMistakes.correction: Correct algebra can still widen the domain: combining logs hides the requirement that EACH original log input be positive. The original equation is the judge — always test candidates in it, and discard what it rejects.

The quotient rule works INSIDE one log ($\log \frac{M}{N} = \log M - \log N$), not across a ratio OF logs. $\frac{\log 20}{\log 3} \approx 2.73$ but $\log \frac{20}{3} \approx 0.82$ — different numbers. A ratio of logs is just division; leave it.

tryIt.statement: Solve $5 \cdot 2^{x} = 80$ two ways: by isolating and matching bases, and state the exact answer.
tryIt.solution: Isolate first: $2^x = 16$. Match: $16 = 2^4$, so $x = 4$. (Log route agrees: $x = \frac{\log 16}{\log 2} = 4$.) Never take logs before isolating — $\log(5 \cdot 2^x)$ needs an extra product-rule step.

tryIt.statement: A city of 100{,}000 grows 4% yearly: $P(t) = 100000(1.04)^t$. Set up the equation for when the population reaches 150{,}000 and express $t$ exactly with logs.
tryIt.solution: $100000(1.04)^t = 150000 \Rightarrow (1.04)^t = 1.5$. Log both sides: $t = \frac{\log 1.5}{\log 1.04} \approx 10.3$ years. Sanity: $(1.04)^{10} \approx 1.48$ — just shy, so a bit over 10 years. ✓

steps.title: The unknown is upstairs
steps.body: Every equation so far had the unknown on the ground floor. Exponential equations hide it UPSTAIRS, in the exponent: $3^x = 20$. Two tools bring it down: matching bases (when both sides are powers of one number) and logarithms (always). This lesson is those two tools plus the log equations that run the other way.

steps.title: Log both sides
steps.body: When 20 refuses to be a power of 3, stop hunting and take a log of BOTH sides: $\log(3^x) = \log 20$. The power rule pulls the prisoner down: $x \log 3 = \log 20$, so $x = \frac{\log 20}{\log 3} \approx 2.727$. Any base works ($\ln$ gives the same $x$); the answer is a ratio of logs — leave it exact, and do NOT merge it into $\log \frac{20}{3}$.

steps.title: Where growth meets a target
steps.teach: The race view: an exponential climbing toward a line. Solving $3^x = 20$ IS finding this crossing — the input where the curve reaches the target height. Logs name that crossing exactly; the graph shows you it exists and is unique (exponentials are one-to-one).

steps.title: Bracket before solving
steps.explanation: $3^2 = 9 < 20 < 27 = 3^3$: the exponent must sit between 2 and 3. Bracketing first makes calculator slips impossible to miss.

steps.title: Log equations: convert and check
steps.body: When the LOG holds the unknown — $\log_2(x + 3) = 4$ — convert to exponential form: $x + 3 = 2^4$, so $x = 13$. But add a mandatory ritual: **check in the original**. Combining logs (product rule) can smuggle in candidates that feed a log a negative number. Those are *extraneous* — algebra ghosts, not solutions.

steps.title: Judge the candidates
steps.prompt: Solving $\log_3(x) + \log_3(x + 2) = 1$ yields candidates $x = 1$ and $x = -3$. The solution set is:

steps.title: Isolate, bracket, solve, check
steps.body: The four-beat rhythm for anything exponential or logarithmic: ISOLATE the power or log first ($5 \cdot 2^x = 80 \to 2^x = 16$), BRACKET the answer between integer powers, SOLVE with matching bases or logs, CHECK in the original (mandatory for log equations). Each beat catches a different classic error.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Same base ⇒ same exponent; rewrite 4, 8, 16… over one base when you can.
Otherwise log both sides and pull the exponent down: x = log(target)/log(base).
Log equations: convert to exponential form, then CHECK — extraneous roots are routine.

practice.statement: Write the model: a forum starts with 800 members and grows 25% per month. Find the membership after 2 months.
A sample decays: $g(0) = 240, g(1) = 60, g(2) = 15$. Find the decay factor, the percent lost per step, and $g(3)$.
practice.solution: Ratio: $\frac{60}{240} = \frac{1}{4}$: factor $b = 0.25$, so 75% lost per step. $g(3) = 15 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = 3.75$.

practice.statement: $3000$ at 6% for 5 years: compare simple interest with monthly compounding (set up both; evaluate the compound factor as $(1.005)^{60} \approx 1.3489$).
practice.solution: Simple: $3000(1 + 0.06 \cdot 5) = 3900$. Monthly: $3000(1.005)^{60} \approx 3000 \cdot 1.3489 = 4046.70$ — compounding earns about $147 more.

practice.statement: Solve $6^x = 50$ exactly and bracket the answer between consecutive integers.
Solve $\log_5(2x - 1) = 2$ and $\log_2(x) + \log_2(x - 2) = 3$, discarding any extraneous roots.
practice.solution: First: $2x - 1 = 25$: $x = 13$ (input $25 > 0$ ✓). Second: $x(x-2) = 8$: $x^2 - 2x - 8 = (x-4)(x+2) = 0$: candidates $4, -2$. $x = 4$: $\log_2 4 + \log_2 2 = 2 + 1 = 3$ ✓; $x = -2$: negative log input — extraneous. Answers: 13 and 4.

testYourself.statement: An investment triples every decade. Express its 10-year factor, its 1-year factor exactly, and decide: does it more than double in 7 years, given $3^{0.7} \approx 2.16$?
testYourself.solution: Decade factor 3; yearly factor $3^{1/10}$. Seven years: $3^{0.7} \approx 2.16 > 2$: yes, it more than doubles.

testYourself.statement: Which pays more on $1000$ over one year: 12% compounded quarterly, or 11.8% compounded monthly? (Factors: $(1.03)^4 = 1.12550881$; $(1.009833...)^{12} \approx 1.12459$.)
testYourself.solution: Quarterly 12%: factor $\approx 1.12551$. Monthly 11.8%: $\approx 1.12459$. The quarterly 12% wins by about $0.92$ per 1000 — a higher rate beat the finer slicing here.

testYourself.statement: Condense to a single logarithm: $3\log_b x - \tfrac{1}{2}\log_b y + \log_b 2$, and verify at $b = 10, x = 10, y = 100$.
